Sighris: So I worked on opening moves and I've improved and now I remind myself to invade before it is too late.

Under the time pressure I failed to see I needed to connect, and my opponent saw it immediately (they probably saw it as, or before, we started filling in the dame and  was just waiting for me to make the mistake. So they won because they were a better player than me. They had almost captured my invasion earlier (which was similiar to my K3-G3-J5 invasion in this game, but I didn't play G2 and instead ran toward the center sooner because I also didn't have a stone at H16 and I was more worried about being cut off).
Sighris: *when I filled in a _dame_ I created an utte-gaeshi situation for myself... (typo correction)

Sighris: I ended up winning two and losing two. So although I was mad at myself at first for not paying attention to something important, I guess I did okay overall, but not good.  ... But I had fun, so I had a good time (two days ago, on Saturday).

C[Sighris: You asked: "" What do you think of my first move? "" I think a 2-4 move (H6 or F8) is too small.

Sighris: For a beginner, playing on the 3rd or 4th row & column is usually best.  Playing Tengen (center spot, on 9x9 it is E5) is considered a good move in 9x9 games, but I think it is not the best move for beginers.
Sighris: On a 19x19 game the most common (best) first moves are either the 4-4 star-point, or a 4-3 point. I think you know that, if not fully at least partially.

Sighris: Those (F6 and G6) are also good first moves for 9x9 and 13x13 games - and if somebody has not yet reached 18-kyu those are probably the best moves for them.  In 9x9 games good players often play Tengen or next to it (like the F6 4-4 point), but I think Tengen (E5) is not the best first move for beginners.

Sighris: And then in the early 90s I found a cafe in Chicago where a few guys, and one woman, played it (mostly Chess was played there) and I played a bunch of games and crawled up to about 15 kyu...
Sighris: Then I bought a few books, and I got to 12kyu... Then I found I could play it on Yahoo:Games and I got to about 11 kyu I'd guess (all of those ranks are guesses).

ErwinKaluza: One of the most important theorems in modern logic states that an axiomatic system (a system in which you ""assume certain things"") will always have something which is true and unprobable
ErwinKaluza: I think knowing the definition of theorem as ""truth deducable from certain assumptions"" is quite useful
ErwinKaluza: I had an argument with a friend of mine just a few days ago while we were having dinner about education and this
ErwinKaluza: He says it would be too ""time expendable"" to build knowledge from the ground up
ErwinKaluza: I think it an ideal scenario people should be able to adapt their education to themselves, so I was being too personal
ErwinKaluza: But I think for someone who wants to ""build and understand science"" it should be better to do so.
ErwinKaluza: Having the ability to create a structure and relate things knowing how they interact seems a lot more fascinating to me than just talking about a couple concepts incompletely
ErwinKaluza: Which I think should also create bad habits on teachers and so
ErwinKaluza: What we do with physics is similar to doing mathematics the other way around
ErwinKaluza: One of hilberts problems (a couple of problems he choose to spark the scientific comunity in the beggining of the 1900's) is about axiomating physics
ErwinKaluza: Finding the simplest assumptions upon which we could get ""what happens""
ErwinKaluza: So I think here as well comparing ""stuctures"" between mathematics and physics should be of the highest value

C[Sighris: The score (point total) is easier to understand if you see the components and how they work together.

Sighris: First off, it seems pretty simple (and mostly is simple - relative to Chess or Poker - about the same as Backgammon) there are 361 points (intersections of two lines) on the 19x19 board and each player gets a point for each point they control = their territory...
Sighris: ...and WeiQi is a ""zero sum game"" meaning every time you capture a point, that point is not available for me/your-opponent.
Sighris: So IF I capture 5 points of territory that you thought you controlled, then you lose those five points and I gain them.
Sighris: Now there is one other thing I should mention, the game WeiQi was invented in China, and later it was played in Japan and Korea
column2: and Japan developed a different way to count the score which almost always gets the same result (who wins, and by how much) as the Chinese system, but in a few very rare circumstances (which almost never happen in beginner games, only in Pro games or Dan-Level games) the Japanes counting system can change the score by a few points... And in pro games they often try to win a game by 1.5 points or even 1/2 a point, so a change of 2 points can change who wins the game
column3: but don't worry about it until you get to a rank of about 7 or 8k.... But you should know the differences in how the score is counted....

column1: Sighris: ...so when you are ready, check this out: https://senseis.xmp.net/?Scoring

Sighris: ...or maybe look at this page first, it is almost the same, and I think it links up to the above page, but it has a different focus or explanation - so read which-ever page is easier for you to understand, or read both: https://senseis.xmp.net/?Counting
Sighris:  - the ""score"" is ""counted"" - so these two topics are strongly related or the same thing.

C[Sighris: The game of WeiQi (aka Go and Baduk) is not about capturing capturing the opponent's stones (playing pieces). The game is about capturing territory. Using the Chinese system of counting we do NOT even keep the captured stones, they are promptly returned to the opponent.
C[Sighris: I like Chinese rules better than Japanese and Korean rules.
C[Sighris: There are less rules under the Chinese rules system... Less ""special rules"".
C[Sighris: ...and that is why I like New Zealand rules a lot... They do not have the ""no suicides (extreme sacrifice) moves allowed"" rule.
C[Sighris: All Go/Baduk/WeiQi rules allow a ""throw in"" move, which is a type of sacrifice move, and is suicidal for the ""throw in"" stone....

C[Sighris: Before I played here, on OGS, I played on KGS and I mostly played games where each player had 30 minutes, plus 5x30 seconds over-time - so the games usually took anywhere from about 40 minutes if somebody resigned earlier (which happened less than 10% I'd guess/estimate) and over an hour if both players went into over-time - and then I would often spend 10 or 15 minutes reviewing the game - so it often ended up taking well over an hour of time, which was nice/fun but now I rarely have an hour to spend....

Sighris: So I've been playing correspondence games here on OGS for a while.... Usually just 4 or 5 games here, and 3-5 correspondence ""Toroidal-Go"" games (size 11x11) on the Little Golem Game Server and that was more than enough. :)

Sighris: But then I saw this OGS tournament, and the live face-to-face Cotsen Go Tournament was cancelled (which I've played in many years before) so I decided to join, thinking I would get one new game at a time, like the Cotsen Go Tournament, so I signed up.... and then I saw another tournament, one which was (or ""is"") slower, called the ""Through the long years"" tournament, so I signed up for that too.....

Sighris: AND then I got 18 new games!!! LOL - 9 in each tournament - and I was a little like ""woah, that is too much!"" and I almost dropped out of one of the tournaments, but I decided to stick it out, in the other tournament I could, if needed, not make a move for two weeks, I get a week added per move I make. :)

Sighris: Anyway, in longer ranked corr. games like this, I do spend a little more time on my moves, considering a few options, but if my life is busy (which it often/usually is) I feel I can't spend as much time as I'd like... so.... for me it depends on work and personal life stuff.

C[Sighris: When I lived in Chicago, I lived most of my life there, I took the train to work 4 or 5 days a week (except in the summer and fall when I often rode my motorcycle to work) but now I live in LA, and most of the time when I go someplace I drive, so I do not have OGS on my phone, I only try to give myself 20 or 30 minutes each day to play Go... and sometimes I let myself have 30 or 40 minutes, but occasionally I have to wait a few days to play if I'm working overtime... So I like to have the flexibility of longer games... Also, longer (slower) games also sometimes give me the chance to think deeper about some moves, I can use the ""Analyze game"" function to check my reading, or just use it to do my reading... So that is nice and helping me to see a little deeper into the depth of this fascinating game. Such simple rules, such complex game!

column1: C[Sighris: Yes/No. Chinese rules (which I think are better, at least for on-line playing) Yes AFAIK/understand. Japanese rules (this game) no because surrounded White group  N13 has no eyes and is dead, so Black group N8 is connected, in theory. HOWEVER you are always encouraged to (carefully) play to force the issue if needed.
Sighris: I think it would play out something like this:
Sighris: IF you had additional liberties which were NOT common liberties with my/Black N8 group then you could force me to fill in the false eye...

Sighris: - but under Japanese rules (and in this case Chinese rules too, sort of) N13 is equally as dead as F10 E7 H4 even though it would be possible (if  playing a faster game, and rushed move /clock prevents you from playing correctly) for Black to play:
Sighris: Agreed? If not, continue the game and see if you can gain a point or two! :)

Sighris: So now this is a type of Life and Death problem.... Questions to ask are 1) Can any group get two eyes? 2) Which groups have the least amount of liberties (open spaces), 3) Which groups can ""run"" and get more liberties/space? & 4) Which groups can be captured (to create more space / liberties for the opponent)?

Sighris: In the upper right corner ( S15 - O15 ) you have 6 separate groups and I have 3 (plus one stone at Q13 which I can either sacrifice or easily connect to my 7 stone group (note that if you try to cut me there you will be reducing your liberties down to only 2 and I can put you into atari right away, so that is probably not a good move for you).

Sighris: Because I less groups there (& better positions) my groups are bigger and thus (usually) stronger. You have 4 two stone groups (one which has only two liberties) and a single stone which is in atari.  You could probably learn some good things by reviewing this corner.  Reviewing games is an excellent way to improve.

C[Sighris: One interesting (to me) thing to note about the 21x21 GoBan is that there is no exact center playing point in-between the star points.
Sighris: Meaning that if you want to play in-between D4 and D11 you have to pick between D7 and D8 BOTH of which are 3 away from one stone and 4 away from the other
Sighris:  - ...but on a normal/conventional 19x19 GoBan the star-points are 6 spaces apart, so there is an exact center playing point - 3 spaces from both star-points/stones. Interesting, right?
Sighris: I think I'll share this thought/observation with the big Go Group on FB and get their thoughts on how this changes the game (in addition to there being significantly more center space).

C[Sighris: ""Atari"" in Go is almost the exact same thing as ""check"" in the game of Chess...

Sighris: So click ""Undo"", then after I approve the Undo it will be your turn again.
C[Sighris: So you know what ""liberties"" are, right?
Sighris: My small two stone group at D5-E5 now have only ONE liberty, at C5, so they are in ""atari""....
Sighris: So on your next move, you can play at C5 and capture them, UNLESS I do something to stop that....
C[Sighris: So I played at C5 to stop you from playing there.
Sighris: BTW, do you know the basics of playing the game of Chess?

C[Sighris: It doesn't matter if you have never played Chess, I only ask because I want to know if you know what ""check"" means in Chess, and to know how much experience you have with abstract strategy board games, like Chess and checkers and other board games.
C[Sighris: In my next move I will play C2 which will put your two stones into ""atari""....
C[Sighris: ...so now, if on my next move I play D1 then your D2 stone will be captured...
Sighris: ...and if on my next move I play B3 then your C3 stone will be captured...
Sighris: Do you understand that? (This is a very basic part of the game.)
C[Sighris: Now your E1 stone is in atari.
C[Sighris: Now your J7 stone is in atari.
C[Sighris: Do you understand a group needs to make ""two eyes"" to ""live""?
C[Sighris: To ""live"" means to avoid being captured (or ""killed"" or ""dying"" / same thing).
C[Sighris:  Now your M4 stone is in atari.
C[Sighris: In a friendly game of Chess, when we can kill the opponent's King in the next move, we say ""check"".
Sighris: In a friendly game of Go, when we can capture the opponent's stone, or group of stones, in the next move, we say ""atari"".
C[Sighris: When you played L5 you played yourself in atari, so that was a bad move.
Sighris: I'm now going to play L8, so ""atari"" on your K8 stone.

C[Sighris: Toroidal Go is played on the surface of a Toroid (a doughnut shape). It can be played on a computer app or simulated on a standard board, but requires imagination on the part of the players to perform an abstract join at the edges. Tactics become more elegant without the need for special border and corner cases (joseki) since a toroidal board has only ""middle"" space.

C[Sighris: Re: time-clock -> You are mostly correct, but not 100% correct, there is at least one clock system (which I believe was called ""hour glass"") where time was transferred from one person to the other.

Sighris: Think if we were playing a game IRL, had we had a sand-filled hour-glass, we start the game with an equal amount of sand in each side. Then we stand it up when the game starts, and the 1st player must move before the sand all goes down and when a move is made the hour-glass is flipped, and the 2nd player must play before all the sand goes down, and so on...

Sighris: Anyways, you now have 3 days, so I think as long as you make a move each day you will always have a 2 day cushion for those days when you are too busy... and I have 6 days, so maybe if you make a move each day your clock will also go up to 6 days, giving you more cushion... but I bet you already know that. :)

Sighris: OK, check this out: if you click on the (i) button on the left of the board (assuming you see the same thing I see) then you get game info, which includes the time info, which is: ""Fischer"" = Clock starts with 3 days and increments by 1 day per move up to a maximum of 1 week.
tquid: Ah, I remembered it as a Fischer clock, which does add time when you make a move.
Sighris: Yes, we are playing with Fischer clock, so now you have 3 Days again (because your last move added a day), so if you play soon, you will have 4 days.

C[Sighris: As you probably know, but we DDK players need reminding, try to avoid making the ""empty triangle shape""... and memorize the basic/smallest living (and dead) shapes... it is not hard, it just comes down to understanding fully how to get two eyes (or how to stop your opponent from getting two eyes, same thing) and you will improve your rank by 2 or 3 levels, if not more. :)
Sighris: If you would like, I can email you a JPG image which sums it up nicely.
tquid: Argh! Knew I was doing it and didn't pay heed to the voice. :(

C[Sighris: You mentioned the ""UNBREAKABLE BAMBOO JOINT"" before, so I know you know some of the good shapes... one of the ways to play better at about the 16kyu range is to look for opponents bad shapes (like empty triangle, or multiple cut points close together) and attack those, and avoid making bad shapes and make good shapes instead. :)

Sighris:  - and be willing to revise your plan as the game changes/unfolds... in other words, do not become overly attached to any stone or group of stones, or any plan to capture a certain area... as they say in poker (or at least that song) you have to know when to fold {and when to hold, and even bluff too (in fast pace games, it is very hard to bluff in slow games, except if you out-rank your opponent by 7 or more ranks)}.

C[HelioSeven: 1) If you did not know, my sensei has been posting his lectures online for a couple months now, you may be interested in them: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCGAASXnrt4FtYfFZ608PqHA/videos

HelioSeven: 2) As far as edu software goes, I am more interested in diagnostic tools than in teaching tools, for a couple of similar reasons. The first being that I think it would be easier to integrate into a current classroom environment, and thus secondly it would an be an easier sell to many current educational institutions. In essence, changing how we measure academic performance is an easier stepping stone than changing what we want taught or how it gets taught. Since today's standardized testing regime is essentially still the old pen-and-paper scantron format in computerized form, I believe there exists a niche market for educational video games with built in telemetry to analyze and assess student learning habits while they play (how long a kid spends on a given problem, how friends share answers, etc). Most of my efforts at the moment are focused on is that telemetric analysis and associated machine learning concepts.

C[Sighris: Studying  joseki will help if you do so with the intent of understanding making good shapes (strong shapes), sacrificing pieces (in exchange for getting influence/territory), and a balanced attack/defense (what is an even trade? what is a bad trade?) and _NOT_ memorizing a few sequences (that is for later, when you get to about 12k).
Sighris: There are too many to memorize, unless your goal is to be a Pro player and this is your full time job.

Sighris: I think you will get more benefit by studying ""Life and Death"" problems.  You should be able to memorize the first 6 basic live shapes (or dead shapes if your opponent gets to the key point first).
Sighris: At the level of 14k you should know that (the above), I would say at 17 or 18k you should have them memorized.
Sighris: At 17k a player should also be able to very quickly know if a group is A) dead, B) 100% alive, C) undetermined - at was is needed to live.
Sighris: oops: C) undetermined - and what is needed to live - meaning get to eyes, or run out to a group of yours which does (or will) have two eyes.

C[Sighris: Re: ""4 stone handicap"" - it is hard to tell at our level for two reasons: 1) a 14k player can play as good as a 10k player (or better) sometimes, especially in a slow game like this, or as a 18k player (or worse) if the stronger player can get them to play quickly (HINT, HINT) and a 9k player also varies in their play, even Dan level players make mistakes (but less of course), and more importantly:

Sighris: 2) The location of the 4 corner star-points are interesting because if not used well they are worthless, or if the weaker player is not willing to give them up (sacrifice them for profit/territory else-where) they can actually cause the player to give the better player MORE territory....

Sighris: Additionally, re: the first point, there are several (many?) skills needed to get beyond the beginner level, and sometimes a player who is ranked two or three ranks lower than a particular opponent can beat them consistently if one of their strengths can be used against their opponents weakness.

Sighris: For example, one player might be good at ""fighting"" (life & death problems) but if their opponent can let them win (small) fights while developing a bigger plan/goal (give up small territories while building walls enclosing larger territories) then they can beat somebody who is ranked higher than them.

Sighris: BTW, I was stuck at about 14 or 15k for a looong time because (back then) I didn't ""study"" (read a book or ask higher level player to play or review my games) and I only played against the same 5 or 6 opponents 90% of the time.

Sighris: I was having fun playing and I thought I could learn just by playing.... and that was how I went from absolute beginner to 15k.... (I did read one book, maybe two, but it was a beginner book)
Sighris: Then when I started playing on the internet and noticing people who went from 18k to 8k relatively quickly I decided to study a little...
Sighris:  - and I was able to improve 3 or 4 ranks relatively quickly... and you can do the same. :)

C[Sighris: REMEMBER the most basic Go lesson / skill ---> You need two eyes to live!  Anytime you have a group you want to keep, you should have an idea of where/how you will get two eyes, or run to a group that does.... When you are close to the wall / edge of the board death can come quickly because there is no place to run.
Sighris: another very basic lesson of Go (and poker, and life) do not waste resources (go stones, money, time, troops) on a completely hopeless endeavor.
Sighris: and it seems I need to repeat this again: do not let yourself be divided!  If you get divided into smaller groups, the smaller groups are weaker...
